author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XVIII. 21.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then came Peter to Him, and said, Lord, how oft shall my brother sin against me, and I forgive him? till seven times?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times, but, Until seventy times seven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Peter supposed he was saying something great, wherefore also as aiming at greatness he added, “Until seven times?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this thing, saith he, which Thou hast commanded to do, how often shall I do?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he forever sins, but forever when reproved repents, how often dost thou command us to bear with this man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For with regard to that other who repents not, neither acknowledges his own faults, Thou hast set a limit, by saying, “Let him be to thee as the heathen and the publican;” but to this no longer so, but Thou hast commanded to accept him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How often then ought I to bear with him, being told his faults, and repenting?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it enough for seven times?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ, the good God, who is loving towards man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “I say not unto thee, until seven times, but, until seventy times seven,” not setting a number here, but what is infinite and perpetual and forever.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even as ten thousand times signifies often, so here too.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by saying, “The barren hath borne seven,”the Scripture means many.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that He hath not limited the forgiveness by a number, but hath declared that it is to be perpetual and forever.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This at least He indicated by the parable that is put after.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that He might not seem to any to enjoin great things and hard to bear, by saying, “Seventy times seven,” He added this parable, at once both leading them on to what He had said, and putting down him who was priding himself upon this, and showing the act was not grievous, but rather very easy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore let me add, He brought forward His own love to man, that by the comparison, as He saith, thou mightest learn, that though thou forgive seventy times seven, though thou continually pardon thy neighbor for absolutely all his sins, as a drop of water to an endless sea, so much, or rather much more, doth thy love to man come short in comparison of the boundless goodness of God, of which thou standest in need, for that thou art to be judged, and to give an account.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He went on to say, “The Kingdom of Heaven is likened unto a certain king, which would take account of his servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when he had begun to reckon, one was brought unto him, which owed him ten thousand talents.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But forasmuch as he had not to pay,he commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and his children, and all that he had.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then after this man had enjoyed the benefit of mercy, he went out, and “took by the throat his fellow-servant, which owed him an hundred pence;”and having by these doings moved his lord, he caused him to cast him again into prison, until he should pay off the whole.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how great the difference between sins against man and against God?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As great as between ten thousand talents, and a hundred pence, or rather even much more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this arises both from the difference of the persons, and the constant succession of our sins.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when a man looks at us, we stand off and shrink from sinning: but when God sees us every day, we do not forbear, but do and speak all things without fear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not hereby alone, but also from the benefit and from the honor of which we have partaken, our sins become more grievous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if ye are desirous to learn how our sins against Him are ten thousand talents, or rather even much more, I will try to show it briefly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I fear lest to them that are inclined to wickedness, and love continually to sin, I should furnish still greater security, or should drive the meeker sort to despair, and they should repeat that saying of the disciples, “who can be saved?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless for all that I will speak, that I may make those that attend more safe, and more meek.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that are incurably diseased, and past feeling, even without these words of mine, do not depart from their own carelessness, and wickedness; and if even from hence they derive greater occasion for contempt, the fault is not in what is said, but in their insensibility; since what is said surely is enough both to restrain those that attend to it, and to prick their hearts; and the meeker sort, when they see on the one hand the greatness of their sins, and learn also on the other hand the power of repentance, will cleave to it the more, wherefore it is needful to speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I will speak then, and will set forth our sins, both wherein we offend against God, and wherein against men, and I will set forth not each person’s own, but what are common; but his own let each one join to them after that from his conscience.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And I will do this, having first set forth the good deeds of God to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then are His good deeds?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He created us when we were not, and made all things for our sakes that are seen, Heaven, sea, air, all that in them is, living creatures, plants, seeds; for we must needs speak briefly for the boundless ocean of the works.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Into us alone of all that are on earth He breathed a living soul such as we have, He planted a garden, He gave a help-meet, He set us over all the brutes, He crowned us with glory and honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After that, when man had been unthankful towards his benefactor, He vouchsafed unto him a greater gift.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For look not to this only, that He cast him out of paradise, but mark also the gain that arose from thence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after having cast him out of paradise, and having wrought those countless good works, and having accomplished His various dispensations, He sent even His own Son for the sake of them that had been benefited by Him and were hating Him, and opened Heaven to us, and unfolded paradise itself, and made us sons, the enemies, the unthankful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore it were even seasonable now to say, “O the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God!”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He gave us also a baptism of the re mission of sins, and a deliverance from vengeance, and an inheritance of a kingdom, and He promised numberless good things on our doing what is right, and stretched forth His hand, and shed abroad His Spirit into our hearts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After so many and such great blessings, what ought to be our disposition; should we indeed, even if each day we died for Him who so loves us, make due recompense, or rather should we repay the smallest portion of the debt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means, for moreover even this again is turned to our advantage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then are we disposed, whose disposition ought to be like this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Each day we insult His law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But be ye not angry, if I let loose my tongue against them that sin, for not you only will I accuse, but myself also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Where then would ye that I should begin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With the slaves, or with the free? with them that serve in the army, or with private persons? with the rulers, or with the subjects? with the women, or with the men? with the aged men, or with the young? with what age? with what race? with what rank? with what pursuit?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Would ye then that I should make the beginning with them that serve as soldiers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What sin then do not these commit every day, insulting, reviling, frantic, making a gain of other men’s calamities, being like wolves, never clear from offenses, unless one might say the sea too was without waves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What passion doth not trouble them? what disease doth not lay siege to their soul?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to their equals they show a jealous disposition, and they envy, and seek after vainglory; and to those that are subject to them, their disposition is covetous; but to them that have suits, and run unto them as to a harbor, their conduct is that of enemies and perjured persons.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How many robberies are there with them!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How many frauds!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How many false accusations, and meannesses! how many servile flatteries!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Come then, let us apply in each case the law of Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “He that saith to his brother, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He that hath looked on a woman to lust after her, hath already committed adultery with her.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Unless one humble himself as the little child, he shall not enter into the Kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these even study haughtiness, becoming towards them that are subject to them, and are delivered into their hands, and who tremble at them, and are afraid of them, more fierce than a wild beast; for Christ’s sake doing nothing, but all things for the belly, for money, for vainglory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Can one indeed reckon up in words the trespass of their actions?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What should one say of their decisions, their laughter, their unseasonable discourses, their filthy language?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But about covetousness one cannot so much as speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For like as the monks on the mountains know not even what covetousness is, so neither do these; but in an opposite way to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they indeed, because of being far removed from the disease, know not the passion, but these, by reason of being exceedingly intoxicated with it, have not so much as a perception how great the evil is.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this vice hath so thrust aside virtue and tyrannises, that it is not accounted so much as a heavy charge with those madmen.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But will ye, that we leave these, and go to others of a gentler kind?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Come then, let us examine the race of workmen and artisans.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these above all seem to live by honest labors, and the sweat of their own brow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these too, when they do not take heed to themselves, gather to themselves many evils from hence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the dishonesty that arises from buying and selling they bring into the work of honest labor, and add oaths, and perjuries, and falsehoods to their covetousness often, and are taken up with worldly things only, and continue riveted to the earth; and while they do all things that they may get money, they do not take much heed that they may impart to the needy, being always desirous to increase their goods.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What should one say of the revilings that are uttered touching such matters, the insults, the loans, the usurious gains, the bargains full of much mean trafficking, the shameless buyings and sellings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But will ye that we leave these too, and go to others who seem to be more just?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who then are they?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They that are possessed of lands, and reap the wealth that springs from the earth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what can be more unjust than these?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if any one were to examine how they treat their wretched and toil-worn laborers, he will see them to be more cruel than savages.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For upon them that are pining with hunger, and toiling throughout all their life, they both impose constant and intolerable payments, and lay on them laborious burdens, and like asses or mules, or rather like stones, do they treat their bodies, allowing them not so much as to draw breath a little, and when the earth yields, and when it doth not yield, they alike wear them out, and grant them no indulgence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what can be more pitiable than this, when after having labored throughout the whole winter, and being consumed with frost and rain, and watchings, they go away with their hands empty, yea moreover in debt, and fearing and dreading more that this famine and shipwreck, the torments of the overlookers,and their dragging them about, and their demands, and their imprisonments, and the services from which no entreaty can deliver them!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why should one speak of the merchandise which they make of them, the sordid gains which they gain by them, by their labors and their sweat filling winepresses, and wine vats, but not suffering them to take home so much as a small measure, but draining off the entire fruits into the casks of their wickedness, and flinging to them for this a little money?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And new kinds of usuries also do they devise, and not lawful even according to the laws of the heathens, and they frame contracts for loans full of many a curse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not the hundredth part of the sum, but the half of the sum they press for and exact; and this when he of whom it is exacted has a wife, is bringing up children, is a human being, and is filling their threshing floor, and their wine-press by his own toils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But none of these things do they consider.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore now it were seasonable to bring forward the prophet and say, “Be astonished, O Heaven, and be horribly afraid, O earth,”to what great brutality hath the race of man been madly carried away!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things I say, not blaming crafts, nor husbandry, nor military service,but ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since Cornelius also was a centurion, and Paul a worker in leather, and after his preaching practised his craft, and David was a king, and Job enjoyed the possession of land and of large revenues, and there was no hindrance hereby to any of these in the way of virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Bearing in mind all these things, and considering the ten thousand talents, let us at least hence hasten to remit to our neighbors their few and trifling debts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For we too have an account to give of the commandments wherewith we have been trusted, and we are not able to pay all, no not whatever we may do.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore God hath given us a way to repayment both ready and easy, and which is able to cancel all these things, I mean, not to be revengeful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then that we may learn this well, let us hear the whole parable, going on regularly through it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For there was brought unto Him,” it saith, “one which owed ten thousand talents, and when he had not to pay, He commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and his children.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, I pray thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not of cruelty, nor of inhumanity (for the loss came back again upon himself, for she too was a slave), but of unspeakable tenderness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is His purpose to alarm him by this threat, that He might bring him to supplication, not that he should be sold.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He had done it for this intent, He would not have consented to his request, neither would He have granted the favor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did He not do this, nor forgive the debt before the account?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Desiring to teach him, from how many obligations He is delivering him, that in this way at least he might become more mild towards his fellow servant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even if when he had learnt the weight of his debt, and the greatness of the forgiveness, he continued taking his fellow-servant by the throat; if He had not disciplined him beforehand with such medicines, to what length of cruelty might he not have gone?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith the other?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And his Lordwas moved with compassion, and loosed him, and forgave him the debt.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou again surpassing benevolence?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The servant asked only for delay and putting off the time, but He gave more than he asked, remission and forgiveness of the entire debt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it had been his will to give it even from the first, but he did not desire the gift to be his only, but also to come of this man’s entreaty, that he might not go away uncrowned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that the whole was of him, although this other fell down to him and prayed, the motive of the forgiveness showed, for “moved with compassion” he forgave him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But still even so he willed that other also to seem to contribute something, that he might not be exceedingly covered with shame, and that he being schooled in his own calamities, might be indulgent to his fellow-servant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Up to this point then this man was good and acceptable; for he confessed, and promised to pay the debt, and fell down before him, and entreated, and condemned his own sins, and knew the greatness of the debt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the sequel is unworthy of his former deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For going out straightway, not after a long time but straightway, having the bene fit freshupon him, he abused to wickedness the gift, even the freedom bestowed on him by his master.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, “he found one of his fellow-servants, which owed him an hundred pence, and took him by the throat, saying, Pay me what thou owest.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the master’s benevolence?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the servant’s cruelty?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear, ye who do these things for money.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if for sins we must not do so, much more not for money.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith the other?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he did not regard even the words by which he had been saved (for he himself on saying this was delivered from the ten thousand talents), and did not recognize so much as the harbor by which he escaped shipwreck; the gesture of supplication did not remind him of his master’s kindness, but he put away from him all these things, from covetousness and cruelty and revenge, and was more fierce than any wild beast, seizing his fellow-servant by the throat.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What doest thou, O man? perceivest thou not, thou art making the demand upon thyself, thou an thrusting the sword into thyself, and revoking the sentence and the gift?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But none of these things did he consider, neither did he remember his own state, neither did he yield; although the entreaty was not for equal objects.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the one besought for ten thousand talents, the other for a hundred pence; the one his fellow-servant, the other his lord; the one received entire forgiveness, the other asked for delay, and not so much as this did he give him, for “he cast him into prison.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But when his fellow-servants saw it, they accused him to their lord.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not even to men is this well-pleasing, much less to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They therefore who did not owe, partook of the grief.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith their lord?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “O thou wicked servant, I forgave thee all that debt, because thou desiredstme; shouldest not thou also have had compassion, even as I had pity on thee?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See again the lord’s gentleness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He pleads with him, and excuses himself, being on the point of revoking his gift; or rather, it was not he that revoked it, but the one who had received it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He saith, “I forgave thee all that debt, because thou desiredst me; shouldest not thou also have had compassion on thy fellow-servant?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even if the thing doth seem to thee hard; yet shouldest thou have looked to the gain, which hath been, which is to be.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Even if the injunction be galling, thou oughtest to consider the reward; neither that he hath grieved thee, but that thou hast provoked God, whom by mere prayer thou hast reconciled.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if even so it be a galling thing to thee to become friends with him who hath grieved thee, to fall into hell is far more grievous; and if thou hadst set this against that, then thou wouldest have known that to forgive is a much lighter thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas, when he owed ten thousand talents, he called him not wicked, neither reproached him, but showed mercy on him; when he had become harsh to his fellow-servant, then he saith, “O thou wicked servant.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us hearken, the covetous, for even to us is the word spoken.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us hearken also, the merciless, and the cruel, for not to others are we cruel, but to ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then thou art minded to be revengeful, consider that against thyself art thou revengeful, not against another; that thou art binding up thine own sins, not thy neighbors.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to thee, whatsoever thou mayest do to this man, thou doest as a man and in the present life, but God not so, but more mightily will He take vengeance on thee, and with the vengeance hereafter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For He delivered him over till he should pay that which was due,” that is, for ever; for he will never repay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since thou art not become better by the kindness shown thee, it remains that by vengeance thou be corrected.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet, “The graces and the gifts are without repentance,”but wickedness has had such power as to set aside even this law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then can be a more grievous thing than to be revengeful, when it appears to overthrow such and so great a gift of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he did not merely “deliver” him, but “was wroth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when he commanded him to be sold, his were not the words of wrath (therefore neither did he do it), but a very great occasion for benevolence; but now the sentence is of much indignation, and vengeance, and punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then means the parable?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “So likewise shall my Father do also unto you,” He saith, “if ye from your hearts forgive not every one his brother their trespasses.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He saith not “your Father,” but “my Father.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not meet for God to be called the Father of such a one, who is so wicked and malicious.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Two things therefore doth He here require, both to condemn ourselves for our sins, and to forgive others; and the former for the sake of the latter, that this may become more easy (for he who considers his own sins is more indulgent to his fellow-servant); and not merely to forgive with the lips, but from the heart.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then thrust the sword into ourselves by being revengeful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what grief hath he who hath grieved thee inflicted upon thee, like thou wilt work unto thyself by keeping thine anger in mind, and drawing upon thyself the sentence from God to condemn thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if indeed thou art watchful, and keepest thyself under control, the evil will come round upon his head, and it will be he that will suffer harm; but if thou shouldest continue indignant, and displeased, then thyself wilt undergo the harm not from him, but from thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Say not then that he insulted thee, and slandered thee, and did unto thee ills beyond number; for the more thou tellest, so much the more dost thou declare him a benefactor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he hath given thee an opportunity to wash away thy sins; so that the greater the injuries he hath done thee, so much more is he become to thee a cause of a greater remission of sins.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we be willing, no one shall be able to injure us, but even our enemies shall advantage us in the greatest degree.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why do I speak of men?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what can be more wicked than the devil; yet nevertheless, even hence have we a great opportunity of approving ourselves; and Job showeth it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if the devil hath become a cause of crowns, why art thou afraid of a man as an enemy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See then how much thou gainest, bearing meekly the spiteful acts of thine enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: First and greatest, deliverance from sins; secondly, fortitude and patience; thirdly, mildness and benevolence; for he that knoweth not how to be angry with them that grieve him, much more will he be ready to serve them that love him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Fourthly, to be free from anger continually, to which nothing can be equal.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of him that is free from anger, it is quite clear that he is delivered also from the despondency hence arising, and will not spend his life on vain labors and sorrows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that knows not how to hate, neither doth he know how to grieve, but will enjoy pleasure, and ten thousand blessings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that we punish ourselves by hating others, even as on the other hand we benefit ourselves by loving them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Besides all these things, thou wilt be an object of veneration even to thy very enemies, though they be devils; or rather, thou wilt not so much as have an enemy whilst thou art of such a disposition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is greater than all, and first, thou gainest the favor of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Shouldest thou have sinned, thou wilt obtain pardon; shouldest thou have done what is right, thou wilt obtain a greater confidence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us accomplish therefore the hating no one, that God also may love us, that, though we be in debt for ten thousand talents, He may have compassion and pity us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But hast thou been injured by him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Pity him then, do not hate him; weep and mourn, do not turn away from him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thou art not the one that hath offended against God, but he; but thou hast even approved thyself, if thou endure it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider that Christ, when about to be crucified, rejoiced for Himself, but wept for them that were crucifying Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This ought to be our disposition also; and the more we are injured, so much the more should we lament for them that are injuring us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to us many are the benefits hence arising, but to them the opposites.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But did he insult thee, and strike thee before all?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then hath he disgraced and dishonored himself before all, and hath opened the mouths of a thousand accusers, and for thee hath he woven more crowns, and gathered for thee many to publish thy forbearance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But did he slander thee to others?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God is the one that is to demand the account, not they that have heard this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to himself hath he added occasion of punishment, so that not only for his own sins he should give account, but also of what he said of thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And upon thee hath he brought evil report with men, but he himself hath incurred evil report with God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if these things are not sufficient for thee, consider that even thy Lordwas evil reported of both by Satan and by men, and that to those most loved by Him; and His Only-Begotten the same again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He said, “If they have called the Master of the house Beelzebub, much more shall they call them of His household.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that wicked demon did not only slander Him, but was also believed, and slandered Him not in ordinary matters, but with the greatest reproaches and accusations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he affirmed Him to be possessed, and to be a deceiver, and an adversary of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But hast thou also done good, and received evil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, in respect of this most of all lament and grieve for him that hath done the wrong, but for thyself rather rejoice, because thou art become like God, “Who maketh the sun to rise upon evil and good.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if to follow God is beyond thee, although to him that watcheth not even this is hard; yet nevertheless if this seem to thee to be too great for thee, come let us bring thee to thy fellow-servants, to Joseph, who suffered countless things, and did good unto his brethren; to Moses, who after their countless plots against him, prayed for them; to the blessed Paul, who cannot so much as number what he suffered from them, and is willing to be accursed for them; to Stephen, who is stoned, and entreating this sin may be forgiven them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And having considered all these things, cast away all anger, that God may forgive us also all our trespasses by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, with whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost be glory, might, honor, now and always, and world without end.
